summary:
The Zapotec writing system (c. 500–400 BC) is among the earliest writing systems in the Americas, developed by the Zapotec civilization centered at Monte Albán in the Valley of Oaxaca, Mexico. Inscriptions on stone monuments (stelae and slabs) at Monte Albán include glyphs representing dates in a 260-day ritual calendar, place names, and what appear to be personal names — making it the oldest known writing in Mesoamerica, predating the better-known Maya script by several centuries.

The Zapotec script is not yet fully deciphered. It appears to be logosyllabic — combining logograms (symbols representing words) with phonetic elements — and shows evidence of a bar-and-dot numerical system shared with later Mesoamerican civilizations. The famous 'danzante' (dancer) monuments at Monte Albán, which show contorted human figures believed to represent defeated enemies or sacrificial victims, include early glyphic inscriptions. The development of writing at Monte Albán coincided with the city's emergence as a dominant political center, suggesting writing was closely tied to political and ritual authority — recording conquests, calendrical observations, and perhaps administrative information, as in the Old World's earliest scripts.
